CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear method–experiments–conclusion flow and thorough related work; the writing quality is good and figures/tables/examples appear effective. However, clarity suffers from missing reporting of hyperparameters and absence of notes on code availability or limitations, which impedes reproducibility and leaves some implementation details ambiguous. Notation is generally consistent and well-defined, but a more explicit accounting of experimental choices (e.g. selection techniques, parameter settings) would improve transparency. From a formalist reviewer’s perspective the presentation is strong but could use a few targeted clarifications to reach excellence. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a geometric, contextually-generated subspace model enabling zero-shot metaphor identification) are stated clearly and early. Notation and methodology (PMI-based vectors, subspace selection, logistic regression classifier) are presented coherently and related work coverage is thorough, making the argument easy to follow. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is good, but the manuscript omits some practical details (e.g. hyperparameters, code release) that would aid reproducibility and slightly detract from presentation completeness.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early, with a clear method–experiments–conclusion flow and thorough related work; the writing quality is good and figures/tables/examples appear effective. However, clarity suffers from missing reporting of hyperparameters and absence of notes on code availability or limitations, which impedes reproducibility and leaves some implementation details ambiguous. Notation is generally consistent and well-defined, but a more explicit accounting of experimental choices (e.g. selection techniques, parameter settings) would improve transparency. From a formalist reviewer’s perspective the presentation is strong but could use a few targeted clarifications to reach excellence.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (a geometric, contextually-generated subspace model enabling zero-shot metaphor identification) are stated clearly and early. Notation and methodology (PMI-based vectors, subspace selection, logistic regression classifier) are presented coherently and related work coverage is thorough, making the argument easy to follow. Figures/tables and examples are described as effective and the writing quality is good, but the manuscript omits some practical details (e.g. hyperparameters, code release) that would aid reproducibility and slightly detract from presentation completeness.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper contributes a notable new perspective by proposing ad-hoc geometric subspaces generated per adjective–noun pair to enable zero-shot metaphor identification using only individual word vectors — a clear methodological departure from Gutiérrez et al. (2016)'s tensor-based supervised and semi‑supervised approaches. The work is differentiated from prior work in its contextual subspace construction and demonstrated competitive performance on the AN adjective–noun dataset. From my formalist stance, I withhold a top rating because the summary indicates no formal theoretical analysis justifying why the geometric/subspace construction captures metaphoricity, which tempers the perceived novelty despite the clear methodological innovation. | B(4): The paper proposes a notable new perspective—generating ad-hoc geometric subspaces per adjective-noun pair to enable zero-shot metaphoricity detection—which is not a standard approach in prior supervised tensor methods. It clearly differentiates itself from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) by relying only on individual word vectors (no phrase training) and demonstrating competitive F-scores with a simple logistic classifier. While the core components (PMI vectors, logistic regression) are familiar, the contextual subspace idea is a non-trivial and practically useful extension that would inform experts interested in resource-light, zero-shot solutions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper contributes a notable new perspective by proposing ad-hoc geometric subspaces generated per adjective–noun pair to enable zero-shot metaphor identification using only individual word vectors — a clear methodological departure from Gutiérrez et al. (2016)'s tensor-based supervised and semi‑supervised approaches. The work is differentiated from prior work in its contextual subspace construction and demonstrated competitive performance on the AN adjective–noun dataset. From my formalist stance, I withhold a top rating because the summary indicates no formal theoretical analysis justifying why the geometric/subspace construction captures metaphoricity, which tempers the perceived novelty despite the clear methodological innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a notable new perspective—generating ad-hoc geometric subspaces per adjective-noun pair to enable zero-shot metaphoricity detection—which is not a standard approach in prior supervised tensor methods. It clearly differentiates itself from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) by relying only on individual word vectors (no phrase training) and demonstrating competitive F-scores with a simple logistic classifier. While the core components (PMI vectors, logistic regression) are familiar, the contextual subspace idea is a non-trivial and practically useful extension that would inform experts interested in resource-light, zero-shot solutions.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a promising geometric subspace approach with useful sensitivity checks and competitive results on the Gutiérrez adjective–noun dataset, but they also agree on key shortcomings: missing theoretical analysis, limited evaluation on a single dataset, lack of reproducibility details (code/hyperparameters), and no discussion of failure cases. Given these solid empirical signs of value but significant gaps that prevent strong claims or easy replication, a middle score of 3 best reflects the paper’s current contributions and limitations.

reviewer_a.justification: From my formalist perspective the paper falls short in several critical dimensions. Although the authors perform multiple selection-technique and window-size comparisons (five subspace selection techniques and varying co-occurrence windows), they present no formal theoretical analysis explaining why the proposed geometric/subspace construction should model metaphoricity, rely on a single adjective–noun corpus (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) for all evaluation, and report classification results only with logistic regression. Key reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters and code not mentioned) and the manuscript omits any discussion of failure cases or limitations, so the experimental evidence is insufficient to fully support the strong zero-shot generalization and state-of-the-art claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments convincingly show the proposed geometric contextual subspace approach can match prior supervised tensors and beat semi-supervised baselines on the Gutiérrez et al. adjective–noun dataset, and the authors test multiple subspace selection methods and window sizes (useful sensitivity checks). However, important reproducibility details are missing (no code release, hyperparameters not reported), only a single dataset is evaluated, and the paper omits discussion of failure cases or limitations — these gaps prevent a higher score from my practical-reproducibility perspective.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree that the paper makes fair and appropriate comparisons to the key prior tensor baselines and simple baselines on the same AN dataset and metric, and that results are clearly reported. However, Reviewer A rightly notes the evaluation is limited to a single adjective–noun corpus and does not include more contemporary or cross‑domain baselines, so while comparisons are sound they are not as comprehensive as they could be—hence a slightly conservative but positive score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to the pertinent Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised and semi‑supervised tensor baselines plus simple baselines (majority, word2vec cosine), and uses the same dataset and F‑score metric, so basic fairness is maintained. However, evaluation is restricted to a single adjective–noun corpus and omits other contemporary metaphor identification methods and multi‑domain benchmarks, limiting the strength of claims about state‑of‑the‑art performance. The authors do report when prior methods perform comparably (noting parity with the supervised tensor), but the paper would be stronger with additional cross‑corpus comparisons and a broader set of baselines.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against the key prior methods (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi‑supervised tensors), a simple majority baseline and a word2vec cosine baseline, using the same Gutiérrez AN dataset and F‑score metric, so comparisons are appropriate and fair. It explicitly reports when performance is comparable to the supervised tensor and superior to the semi‑supervised variant, and the related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating relevant competing approaches are cited and discussed. From a practical standpoint this is a comprehensive and fair empirical comparison.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is on‑topic and experimentally compares to relevant baselines on a standard adjective–noun dataset, but they also both note shortcomings in reproducibility (missing code/hyperparameters) and limited evaluation/theoretical justification. Given these substantive concerns about generality and experimental rigor that would need addressing for a top venue, I assign a score of 3 while acknowledging the method and results are promising.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—metaphor identification via distributional/geometric methods—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and the paper compares against established baselines on an appropriate adjective–noun task. However, from the provided summary I must penalise the work for relying on a single dataset (Gutiérrez et al. 2016) and for lacking formal theoretical justification for why the proposed subspace construction captures metaphoricity; these are important for generality and conceptual contribution. The experimental regime also appears limited: classification uses only logistic regression (a shallow classifier) and important reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are missing, which reduces its fit to a top-tier venue without substantial revision.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the NLP/ML conference scope: it proposes a distributional-geometric model for metaphor identification, evaluates on a standard adjective–noun dataset (Gutiérrez et al. 2016, 8,592 dyads), and compares to relevant baselines including supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods. The approach is methodologically appropriate (uses PMI vectors from Wikipedia, ad‑hoc subspace selection, and logistic regression) and achieves competitive F‑scores, which will interest the venue’s audience focused on practical, reproducible modelling and zero‑shot generalisation. My main concerns are missing reproducibility details (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported) and limited discussion of broader limitations, which are minor but relevant for a top venue’s standards.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments provide plausible empirical support for the zero‑shot metaphor identification claim and that the approach is simple and resource‑efficient, but they also concur on notable methodological shortcomings (missing code/hyperparameters, single corpus evaluation, no statistical significance tests). Reviewer A further flags the lack of a formal theoretical justification as a substantive gap. Given these reproducibility and theoretical issues that limit confidence in the technical claims, a moderate soundness score of 3 is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims and presents experimental evidence comparing the geometric subspace model to Gutiérrez et al. (2016) baselines on the adjective–noun dataset, supporting the zero-shot/metaphor identification claims to an extent. However, I find methodological gaps: no hyperparameters or code are reported, evaluation is limited to a single corpus (no cross-corpus validation), and classifiers are limited to logistic regression despite claims about generality. Crucially, the paper lacks a formal theoretical justification for why the chosen geometric/subspace construction should capture metaphoricity and does not report statistical significance tests, so the technical claims are promising but incompletely supported.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claim—that a contextual geometric subspace model enables zero-shot identification of unseen adjective–noun metaphors—is supported by experiments on the Gutiérrez et al. dataset and direct comparisons to their supervised and semi‑supervised tensor baselines, with competitive F‑scores. The methodological approach is simple and plausible (uses PMI vectors and logistic regression) and aligns with my preference for resource‑efficient models, but the paper omits key reproducibility details (no code mentioned, hyperparameters not reported) and does not report statistical significance testing, which are minor but important gaps that prevent a full technical endorsement.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical problem with a lightweight, interpretable geometric approach that achieves strong results on a standard adjective–noun dataset. The main limitations are the lack of theoretical justification, single‑corpus evaluation, and incomplete reproduction details, which constrain generality but do not negate the clear practical value and likely follow‑on impact; for these reasons I rate impact a 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets the important problem of metaphor identification and proposes a simple, general geometric contextual subspace method that attains state-of-the-art F-scores on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective–noun dataset using only word vectors, which could pragmatically influence representation choices. However, from my formalist perspective its impact is constrained: the work lacks formal theoretical justification for why the subspace construction should capture metaphoricity and evaluates on a single corpus (no cross‑corpus validation), limiting confidence in generality. While the method’s zero‑shot promise and multiple subspace selection variants suggest new directions, the absence of diverse classifiers, detailed hyperparameter reporting, and broader empirical validation reduces its likelihood of substantially reshaping the field without follow‑up work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important, practical problem—identifying unseen metaphors—using a simple, resource-light method that only requires word vectors and achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor baseline on a standard adjective–noun dataset (Gutiérrez et al. 2016). From my perspective, demonstrating zero-shot generalisation with an interpretable geometric, context-specific subspace is likely to change how researchers approach metaphoricity (favoring lightweight, adaptable models) and opens clear follow-ups (broader datasets, release of code/hyperparameters, and extensions to other phrase types). It is not fully transformative because evaluation is limited to one dataset and reproduction details aren’t reported, but it offers strong, practical value and will likely be built upon.

main_claims: Proposes a geometric, contextually-generated subspace model that projects each candidate adjective-noun pair into an ad-hoc vector space for metaphor identification.
Model requires only individual word vectors (not phrase representations), enabling zero-shot identification of novel/unseen metaphors.
When analysed with a logistic regression the model achieves F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method of Gutiérrez et al. (2016) and superior to their semi-supervised version.
The method is simple and general, and shown to obtain state-of-the-art performance on the tested adjective-noun metaphoricity task.
_summary.methodology: Starting from PMI-based distributional vectors computed from the December 2014 English Wikipedia dump, the method selects k-dimensional subspaces for each candidate adjective-noun pair (five selection techniques and varying co-occurrence windows were tested) to produce contextual geometric representations, which are then classified (via logistic regression) as metaphoric or literal.
datasets: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset (8,592 dyads, 23 adjectives)
baselines: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised tensor method (G2016 V1)
Gutiérrez et al. (2016) semi-supervised tensor method (G2016 V2)

reviewer_a.name: Formalist Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Senior computational linguist with a tenured background in formal semantics and mathematical models of language; long-standing reviewer for ACL and TACL focusing on conceptual rigor.
traits: Penalises papers that do not provide a formal theoretical analysis or justification for why the proposed geometric/subspace construction should capture metaphoricity (subtracts points if only empirical motivation is given).
Penalises papers that evaluate only on a single corpus or single dataset variant (subtracts points if no cross-corpus or multi-domain validation is present).
Penalises papers that lack detailed ablation studies isolating the contribution of each design choice (subtracts points if fewer than 3 ablations or no statistical significance tests).

Penalises papers that rely solely on shallow classifiers (e.g., logistic regression) without discussing the interaction between representation and classifier choice (subtracts points if no experiments with at least one qualitatively different classifier).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritises theoretical justification and thorough controlled analyses; will downgrade empirical-only contributions that lack formal grounding or comprehensive ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Practical Generalist
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with experience deploying language-understanding components and evaluating zero-shot and few-shot methods; frequent reviewer for EMNLP and NeurIPS workshops.
traits: Rewards papers that introduce simple, general, and reproducible methods enabling zero-shot or unseen-item handling (adds points for methods that require only standard word vectors and no phrase-level training).
Rewards strong empirical performance and clear benchmarking against prior SOTA on the task (adds points if reported F-scores match or exceed baselines with comparable experimental conditions).
Rewards methodological simplicity and resource-efficiency (adds points for approaches that use lightweight classifiers like logistic regression and publicly available corpora/vectors).
Penalises papers that do not release code or sufficient experimental details for reproduction (subtracts points if training details, hyperparameters, or data processing steps are missing).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Values practical impact, simplicity, and reproducibility; will rate methods that enable zero-shot generalisation and match SOTA more favorably even if formal theory is limited.
